event: The Last Judgment at the end of time, when Christ enthroned in glory separates the righteous from the wicked
symbols: Christ enthroned at the summit of the composition in radiant glory — the Son of Man coming in his glory to judge the living and the dead, seated upon the throne of his majesty as foretold in Matthew 25:31

the Virgin Mary and Saint John the Baptist flanking Christ in the Deesis arrangement — the two principal intercessors of Christian tradition, representing the fullness of humanity's plea before the divine Judge

the large wooden cross held aloft at the center of the composition — the instrument of salvation displayed as the standard of judgment, the sign of the Son of Man appearing in heaven (Matthew 24:30), by which all humanity is measured
angels with trumpets summoning the dead — the great trumpet blast of the last day (1 Thessalonians 4:16), the angelic ministers executing God's final decree
the separation of figures into upper heavenly realm and lower earthly chaos — the division of sheep from goats, the righteous ascending to glory while the condemned are cast down into perdition
saints and patriarchs arrayed in clouds around the throne — the great cloud of witnesses (Hebrews 12:1), the communion of saints gathered in heavenly worship at the consummation of all things
naked and exposed figures at the bottom of the composition — the dead raised from their graves, stripped of all earthly pretension, standing vulnerable before the all-seeing Judge
description: A monumental vertical composition depicting the Last Judgment in the Counter-Reformation Baroque style. At the apex, Christ sits enthroned in brilliant golden light, flanked by the Virgin Mary and Saint John the Baptist in the traditional Deesis arrangement. Below them, a large wooden cross is held upright at the center by angelic figures. Rows of saints, apostles, and patriarchs in richly colored robes are arranged in concentric cloud-banks around the heavenly court. In the middle register, angels with outstretched wings act as agents of divine justice. At the bottom, newly risen figures in various states of nakedness emerge from the earth, some being drawn upward toward salvation, others falling or being driven downward toward damnation. The palette is warm and luminous in the heavenly zone, darkening toward turbulence at the base.
theological_description: Francisco Pacheco's Last Judgment of 1614 represents one of the great eschatological subjects of Christian art, the culminating event of salvation history when Christ returns in glory to render final judgment upon all humanity. This painting faithfully follows the Western iconographic tradition established in medieval tympana and brought to its supreme expression in Michelangelo's Sistine Chapel fresco, yet inflects it through the lens of Spanish Counter-Reformation piety. The tripartite vertical structure -- heavenly court above, angelic mediation in the middle, and the tumult of resurrection and judgment below -- mirrors the theological reality that the Last Judgment is simultaneously an act of divine sovereignty, angelic ministry, and human reckoning. Christ at the summit is the fulfillment of his own prophecy: 'When the Son of Man comes in his glory, and all the angels with him, then he will sit on his glorious throne. Before him will be gathered all the nations, and he will separate people one from another as a shepherd separates the sheep from the goats' (Matthew 25:31-32). The prominence of the cross at the center is profoundly significant: it is not merely a decorative element but the criterion of judgment itself. The cross is the sign by which all human history is evaluated -- those who embraced its scandal are saved, those who rejected it are condemned. As Paul wrote, 'the word of the cross is folly to those who are perishing, but to us who are being saved it is the power of God' (1 Corinthians 1:18). The Deesis composition -- Mary and the Baptist flanking Christ -- expresses the ancient Christian conviction that even at the moment of judgment, intercession continues; the Mother of God and the greatest born of women plead on behalf of trembling humanity. The saints arrayed in glory around the throne are not passive spectators but active participants in the judicial act: 'Do you not know that the saints will judge the world?' (1 Corinthians 6:2). The naked figures rising from the earth below embody the doctrine of bodily resurrection -- the dead do not remain dust but are reconstituted in their bodies to face the Judge, for 'we must all appear before the judgment seat of Christ, so that each one may receive what is due for what he has done in the body, whether good or evil' (2 Corinthians 5:10). Pacheco, who was also the chief censor of religious art for the Inquisition in Seville and the author of 'Arte de la Pintura,' painted this work with deliberate theological precision, ensuring that every detail conforms to orthodox Catholic doctrine regarding the Four Last Things: death, judgment, heaven, and hell.

scripture_refs.reason: When the Son of Man comes in his glory he will sit on his throne and separate the sheep from the goats -- the central biblical text for the Last Judgment, directly depicted in the painting's composition
The great white throne judgment and the opening of the books, the dead judged according to what they had done -- the apocalyptic vision of final judgment that underlies the entire scene

The sign of the Son of Man will appear in heaven, and he will send his angels with a loud trumpet call to gather his elect -- the cross displayed in heaven and the angelic trumpeters depicted in the painting
The word of the cross is folly to those who are perishing, but to us who are being saved it is the power of God -- the cross held aloft at the center as the standard by which humanity is judged
We must all appear before the judgment seat of Christ, so that each one may receive what is due for what he has done in the body -- the naked risen figures standing before Christ in judgment
The Lord himself will descend from heaven with a cry of command, with the voice of an archangel, and with the sound of the trumpet of God -- the angels sounding the trumpet of the last day
Do you not know that the saints will judge the world -- the saints arrayed around the throne as participants in the act of judgment
Since we are surrounded by so great a cloud of witnesses -- the saints and patriarchs gathered in the heavenly clouds around the throne of Christ
Thrones were placed and the Ancient of Days took his seat; the court sat in judgment and the books were opened -- the Old Testament prophetic vision of the heavenly tribunal

The hour is coming when all who are in the tombs will hear his voice and come out, those who have done good to the resurrection of life and those who have done evil to the resurrection of judgment -- the bodily resurrection of the dead depicted at the bottom of the painting
